description: Behavior-preserving refinement of the update/marker machinery. (1) Extract the triplicated in-memory migration-registry seeding into one helper in migration-registry-loader.ts — buildFreshRegistryWithChain(substrateDir, chain): substrate on-disk decls + the extra chain decls, deduped on (schemaName, fromVersion) with the substrate decl winning, cache-untouched — and swap the three call sites (simulateResyncOutcome, validateBlockItemsAgainstCatalog, resolveBlocked). The on-disk appendMigrationDeclForDir loops and the live path's cache-warming getProjectMigrationRegistryForDir are a different operation and stay untouched. (2) Hoist the marker sentinel-line pattern (/^(<{7}|>{7})/, inlined at four detection/strip sites) into one named module constant kept in lockstep with the tokens composeMarkerText emits.
acceptance_criteria: buildFreshRegistryWithChain lives in migration-registry-loader.ts beside buildRegistryFromSubstrateForDir; the three former seeding blocks in index.ts call it; no in-memory createRegistry + seed-merge block remains at a call site.

The helper preserves the exact semantics: existing substrate decls first (substrate wins on a key collision), dedup key `${schemaName} ${fromVersion}`, migrationFnFor per decl, no cache warming, no disk writes.
The on-disk register loops in resyncSchema and resolveBlocked and the live getProjectMigrationRegistryForDir call are byte-unchanged.

The marker sentinel pattern is defined once as a named constant used by every detection and strip site; composeMarkerText's emitted sentinel tokens and the constant cannot drift independently (one source).

Behavior-preserving: the full test suite passes UNCHANGED (no test edits needed); build + check + parity-check green; a runtime blocked -> markers -> fix -> resolve-blocked -> in-sync loop still converges.
Fresh adversarial probe confirms zero behavioral drift (the three registry paths, the marker detect/strip sites).
notes: Refinement debt surfaced by the post-TASK-052 code-simplifier evaluation and confirmed by investigation: the three seeding blocks are behaviorally identical (same dedup, same substrate-wins precedence, same error posture; the loop-vs-spread spelling is cosmetic); the sentinel regex is the invariant most likely to break silently under future edits. Internal-only — no exported-surface, op, or schema change.
